items.title: New physics-based metric improves estimates of climate-driven cooling demand
items.summary: New research led by atmospheric scientists at the University of Hawai'i (UH) at Mānoa found that the century-old yardstick used across many industries to estimate air-conditioning and refrigeration energy demand—called cooling degree days—is missing something fundamental.

items.title: Melts in your mouth, but not in your car: The ongoing scientific quest to create heat‑resistant chocolate
items.summary: There are few pleasures greater than letting a piece of chocolate melt slowly on your tongue. Indeed, the proximity of chocolate's melting point to our own body temperature is one reason it is so widely appreciated.

items.title: First 3D nanoscale maps of human airway epithelium reveal hidden architecture of lung defense cells
items.summary: Researchers at Queen Mary University of London and University of Cambridge in collaboration with colleagues at HHMI Janelia and NIH, have created some of the most detailed three-dimensional maps ever generated of human airway cells, revealing how the specialized cells that protect our lungs are buil

items.title: Lithium ions sprint up to 10,000 times faster when their molecular cages briefly open
items.summary: An international collaborative research group led by Professor Bong June Sung of the Department of Chemistry at Sogang University and Professor Shinji Saito of the Institute for Molecular Science (IMS), National Institutes of Natural Sciences (NINS), and the Graduate University for Advanced Studies,

items.title: Neutral catalysts achieve chalcogen-bonding asymmetry
items.summary: Chemists have demonstrated that neutral chalcogen-bond donors can induce asymmetry in chemical reactions, addressing a challenge that has limited the development of chalcogen-bonding catalysis.

items.title: SAD je ospice iskorijenio 2000. godine. Broj slučajeva je opet eksplodirao
items.summary: SAD JE NA rubu gubitka statusa zemlje bez ospica zbog pada procijepljenosti i širenja dezinformacija. Stručnjaci upozoravaju da je za preokret potrebno vratiti povjerenje u cijepljenje.

items.title: Zebrafish rebuild damaged 'second brain' networks in nine days, offering clues for nerve repair
items.summary: Why can zebrafish regenerate damaged gut nerves while humans largely cannot? Michigan State University researchers have developed a new research tool that allows them to remove gut neurons in zebrafish and watch them regenerate in real time, helping scientists investigate one of regenerative biology

items.title: Yeast survives after all 300 known spliceosomal introns are removed
items.summary: Spliceosomal introns are noncoding DNA sequences located between exons in many eukaryotic protein-coding genes. Most are removed post-transcriptionally by RNA splicing and do not encode proteins, but they have been implicated in various regulatory processes, including gene transcription, mRNA nuclea

items.title: Trace DNA reveals critically endangered Chinese bahaba in Hong Kong after decade without records
items.summary: The Chinese bahaba (Bahaba taipingensis), a marine fish species endemic to the Chinese coast and known as the "Panda of the Sea," is critically endangered and one of the few marine fish listed as a first-class protected wild animal in China. Collaborative research led by the Division of Science at L

items.title: Look for NASA+ Now Streaming on Amazon Fire TV
items.summary: Continuing agency efforts to bring space closer to home, NASA+ is heading to more streaming platforms. On Thursday, NASA announced its programming is on Fire TV Channels. Fire TV customers can easily access this content by asking Alexa+ on compatible devices. Future programming on Fire TV may includ

items.title: Nanopores can activate human T cells without biochemical signals
items.summary: Immune cells protect and heal the human body. In medicine, they are specifically activated through biochemical reactions to treat certain diseases. Researchers at the Helmholtz-Zentrum Hereon, ETH Zurich, Humboldt University of Berlin, Charité Berlin and Inselspital Bern have now discovered that imm

items.title: How AI is helping states cut through decades of red tape
items.summary: Bureaucracy moves slowly, and decades of outdated provisions clogging the bylaws of cities and states only make it worse. Sometimes dubbed "policy sludge," such obsolete processes and stale reporting requirements can trap civil servants in red tape and cause program dysfunction.

items.title: Less cannabis use among adolescents, but social disparities persist
items.summary: Past studies have shown that cannabis use during adolescence is associated with a variety of negative impacts—including emotional distress, adverse academic outcomes, and brain development and functioning—that can persist into adulthood. Though there has been an overall decrease in cannabis use amon

items.title: Virtual café experiment finds Japanese participants keep greater distance than Italians
items.summary: How close do we stand to other people during a social interaction? The distance we maintain between our body and that of another person—known as interpersonal distance—is a fundamental aspect of everyday life. It influences our sense of comfort, safety, intimacy and tendency to approach or avoid oth

items.title: Quantum Newton's cradle set to level up computing
items.summary: Sending quantum information through a chain of qubits, like energy through a Newton's cradle, could be the key to faster operations and take quantum computing to the next level.

items.title: Recycling does not weaken support for broader climate action, survey finds
items.summary: In research published in npj Climate Action, UNSW Sydney behavioral scientists, in collaboration with researchers from Griffith University, say they found no evidence for claims that climate action at the individual level distracted from support for meaningful action on climate change.

items.title: Human impacts rewrite the rules of island biodiversity around the globe
items.summary: A new study reveals that human activities have fundamentally reshaped biodiversity patterns on islands across the globe, with important implications for how scientists understand ecology, evolution and conservation.

items.title: How insect submariners survive the depths without imploding
items.summary: Deep-diving insect larvae are challenging a long-held idea about why there are almost no insects in the ocean, thanks to sturdy air sacs. In Lake Malawi in East Africa, billions of lake fly larvae (Chaoborus edulis) make an unusual commute: By day, they sink more than 200 meters (656 feet) into the

items.title: Silkworm cultivation predates the Silk Road in southern Central Asia, ancient cocoons suggest
items.summary: The silkworm moth, whose caterpillars have been used in silk production since antiquity, may have been brought to southern Central Asia 4,000 years ago—nearly 2,000 years earlier than previously assumed—according to new research from Washington University in St. Louis.

items.title: Hundreds hospitalized in Japan heat wave 'disaster'
items.summary: More than 450 people received hospital treatment for heat-related issues in a single day in Tokyo—the highest number since records began 16 years ago, emergency services said Thursday.

items.title: Hawai'i serves as model for Western researchers studying the moon's volcanic history
items.summary: The moon is 384,000 kilometers (239,000 miles) away from Earth, but one of the best places to study its surface is much closer to home. Western planetary scientist Catherine Neish and PhD candidate Sashank Vanga traveled to Hawai'i Volcanoes National Park in June to investigate lava flows that

items.title: Modern forensics uncovers new clues about Inca sacrifice victims' last days
items.summary: Human sacrifice was deeply ingrained in Inca civilization. Children and young people were selected for sacrifice to appease the gods and sacred mountain spirits. Being chosen was considered a great honor for both the victims and their families. Now, research into the remains of three individuals sac

items.title: Knowing a manager's stance on unions can affect worker performance
items.summary: It's no secret that management at major firms such as Starbucks and Tesla isn't exactly a fan of unionization. But for other companies, how does an anti-union stance affect worker output and performance? It might depend on whether that stance is, in fact, a secret.

items.title: Sweden to ban 'forever chemicals' in clothing
items.summary: Sweden said Thursday it plans to ban so-called "forever chemicals" in consumer products such as clothing and kitchenware as early as 2028, before expected European Union regulations come into force.

items.title: Fields medal awarded to four young mathematicians
items.summary: The Fields Medal, known as the Nobel Prize of mathematics, was awarded Thursday to four people—two Chinese citizens, one American and one Canadian.

items.title: How the encephalomyocarditis virus hijacks host protein machinery
items.summary: Viruses lack the molecular machinery required for protein synthesis. Instead, they hijack the host's protein-making machinery, specifically ribosomes and translational factors. This process is essential for viral multiplication and infection. Viruses have RNA genomes or mRNAs containing structu

items.title: Environmental bacteria ferry genes that boost antibiotic resistance over 10,000-fold
items.summary: A patient with a severe infection is prescribed a potent antibiotic. Hopes for improvement are high, but just after treatment is given, resistance to the drug increases thousands of times. How could this happen?

items.title: Coal-fired power generation rising globally on Mideast war: IEA
items.summary: Coal-fired power generation is set to increase in 2026, driven by supply difficulties and soaring natural gas prices linked to the war in the Middle East, the International Energy Agency said Thursday.

items.title: Researchers examine social media's impact on narcissistic traits
items.summary: Researchers at the University of Huddersfield have developed a new theoretical framework exploring whether social media recommendation systems may contribute to the amplification of narcissistic traits over time.

items.title: Heat is coming for Hawaiian corals, but study finds there may be time to adapt
items.summary: Since the first major bleaching event in 1996, Hawaiian corals have been subject to increasing heat stress and more frequent bleaching events. New research from oceanographers at the University of Hawai'i at Mānoa used high-resolution modeling that includes small-scale processes to examine proj

items.title: Training microbes to prepare soils for the impacts of climate change
items.summary: Scientists at the University of Lincoln have discovered a promising new way to help protect crops from one of climate change's growing threats to global food production—increasing soil salinity.

items.title: How mothers speak has little impact on children's school grades, study reveals
items.summary: A mother's everyday speech habits have little bearing on her child's academic success, according to new research led by the University of York.

items.title: Apprentices in private, independent companies face disadvantages
items.summary: Apprentices in private, independent companies are more likely to fail their final exams, change contracts and not complete their training than apprentices who have contracts through a training agency.

items.title: Ceramic vessels reveal how social inequalities were also cooked into 19th-century Brazil
items.summary: Fish, shellfish, maize, manioc, rice and even chicken eggs. Molecular residues preserved in more than 180 ceramic vessels from southern Brazil have made it possible to reconstruct what different communities were cooking more than a century ago, showing that access to food varied according to the ori

items.title: More than half of Earth's migratory bird species are declining—here's how we can still save them
items.summary: Every year, billions of migratory birds connect the world's continents, oceans and ecosystems as they undertake epic journeys between their foraging habitats and breeding grounds.

items.title: Lipid nanoparticles deliver one-two punch to shrink resistant oral tumors in preclinical models
items.summary: Oral squamous cell carcinoma (OSCC) is the most common form of head and neck cancer, and the number of new cases is predicted to rise by 30% in the next decade. Despite decades of cancer research, treating OSCC remains a challenge. The five-year survival rate is approximately 50%, and standard treat

items.title: Drought, water shortages and crop failures: How does the medieval warm period compare with today?
items.summary: A heat wave lasting four months, followed by water shortages and crop failures. This is England in the 13th century, not the 21st. What is often called the Medieval Warm Period saw elevated temperatures in many parts of the world between the 10th and 13th centuries.

items.title: Most powerful Northern Lights in a generation expose weaknesses in UK infrastructure
items.summary: Millions of people across the UK marveled at the spectacular northern lights that lit up the night sky in May 2024. But while the display captivated skywatchers, the geomagnetic storm behind it also served as the biggest test in a generation of the UK's preparedness for severe space weather. So

items.title: Crews Move Artemis IV Liquid Hydrogen Tank
items.summary: Crews at NASA’s Michoud Assembly Facility in New Orleans transport the 130-foot-tall Artemis IV liquid hydrogen tank out of a production cell inside the main factory building into a detached test building on a separate portion of the 829-acre site on May 15, 2026. The liquid hydrogen tank will form

items.title: Peptide isolated from a Cerrado frog increases strawberry shelf life
items.summary: Five minutes. That was the amount of time that Oso Grande strawberries remained in contact with the Ctx(Ile21)-Ha peptide—a molecule isolated from a frog native to the Brazilian Cerrado—in experiments conducted in Brazil by scientists at the Tupã campus of São Paulo State University (UNESP). It was

items.title: Chasing subseasonal forecasts with AI
items.summary: Subseasonal forecasting, or the ability to predict weather trends two weeks to two months in advance, is a capability highly sought after in various economic sectors, including energy, water management and agriculture. Reliable forecasts on this scale have thus far been largely out of reach, but res

items.title: NASA’s MAVEN Illuminates New Understanding of Auroras at Mars
items.summary: NASA MAVEN (Mars Atmosphere and Volatile Evolution) mission scientists have uncovered a key puzzle piece in understanding certain types of auroras on Mars, finding that they form in a similar way to Earth-based auroras. Results published Thursday in Nature Communications show the same mechanism that

items.title: Would you trust AI for ethical advice?
items.summary: Most people would not prefer to take ethical advice from a computer, but a new Wharton study shows how attitudes change when users see how good AI guidance can be.

items.title: Moon baboon: New insight into the mind of ancient Egyptians
items.summary: New research from the University of St Andrews has shed light on baboons, moonlight and their link to an ancient Egyptian deity.

items.title: Goli sljepaši žive u kolonijama. Kraljica mirisom održava red i neplodnost
items.summary: NOVO istraživanje otkriva kako kraljica golih sljepaša vlada kolonijom. Ona ispušta kemijski spoj koji djeluje kao feromon, sprječavajući druge ženke da se razmnožavaju i održavajući mir.

items.title: From one frontier to another: The quantum revolution
items.summary: Manchester's quantum researchers are building on the Ferranti Mark I legacy, using ultra-pure silicon and single atoms to move quantum computing closer to real-world impact.

items.title: How much should you reveal about yourself on your dating profile? Scientists have found the 'sweet spot'
items.summary: Dating has entered a new era in which finding a potential soulmate is increasingly shaped by the digital world and, often, by algorithms. On dating apps, the way people present themselves through their profiles can significantly influence how approachable, trustworthy and likable they appear.

items.title: Researchers find college-educated population more likely to shelter during dangerous wildfire smoke
items.summary: American adults with college degrees now outlive those without degrees by more than eight years, in part because graduates appear to be more proactive about protecting their health. For example, they smoke cigarettes at far lower rates and follow cancer screening advice more often, especially after

items.title: Shaking atoms to bring black-hole quantum chaos into the lab
items.summary: Physicists have discovered a surprisingly simple way to reproduce one of the most fascinating models in modern physics—linked to black holes, quantum chaos and exotic electronic materials—using ultracold atoms trapped in light.

items.title: Molecular clock transitions tune out the noise in the hunt for new physics
items.summary: Heavy polar molecules are some of the most sensitive tools physicists have for probing what lies beyond the Standard Model, the theory that describes the particles and forces we know about. But turning that sensitivity into precise, trustworthy measurements has long been held back by one stubborn pr

items.title: Predicting where wildfire smoke will go next
items.summary: In recent summers, wildfire smoke has been inescapable across Wisconsin, drifting south from Canada and leading to long stretches of poor air quality. Whether that smoke clears or lingers is often influenced in part by the atmospheric boundary layer—the lowest part of the atmosphere, where airborne

items.title: NASA Astronaut Chris Williams Closes Out Space Station Mission
items.summary: After eight months aboard the International Space Station for his first mission, NASA astronaut Chris Williams is preparing to return to Earth. During his assignment, Williams contributed to research for new cancer treatments, advanced the production of materials to improve computers and electronics

items.title: Orcas filmed smashing giant sunfish into thousands of pieces
items.summary: Researchers in the Gulf of California have documented a dramatic orca behavior never described before. In two separate encounters, one orca gripped a dead sunfish by the fin while another charged into it at high speed, blasting the carcass into thousands of pieces.

items.title: Fig-leaf enzyme turns tannery waste into high-value collagen
items.summary: Tanneries generate large quantities of skin trimmings before hides are tanned. These trimmings contain collagen but are commonly treated as low-value waste. An international research team has developed a method that uses ficin, a protein-digesting enzyme obtained from fig leaves, to recover type I c

items.title: Older teens entering care often overlooked
items.summary: There is an urgent need for better support for older teenagers entering care for the first time, new Griffith University research has found. Professor Silke Meyer from Griffith's School of Allied Health, Sport and Social Work conducted a scoping review that examined research on older adolescent

items.title: No perfect job? Moving forward despite uncertainty may make work more meaningful
items.summary: People who accept that there may not always be a perfect career choice and learn to make decisions despite uncertainty are more likely to find their work meaningful, satisfying and engaging, according to research published in the Journal of Counseling Psychology.

items.title: New teaching method could help Aboriginal languages survive and thrive
items.summary: Curtin University-led research has unveiled a new teaching approach that could help incorporate Aboriginal languages into Australian classrooms, teach younger generations and potentially prevent important cultural connections from being lost.

items.title: Popular childhood drinks may raise blood pressure decades later
items.summary: A 25-year study found that frequent consumption of soda, sports drinks, and fruit juice beginning in childhood was linked to a higher risk of high blood pressure in adulthood. Swapping one daily drink for whole fruit, milk, or water could significantly reduce that risk.

items.title: Soil organic carbon increases crop yield but can't help with extreme drought
items.summary: In cases of extreme drought, soil organic carbon does not improve crop yields, Colorado State University research has found. The finding contradicts the commonly held belief that soil organic carbon benefits yields most during drought.

items.title: Student research shows positive outcomes from summer research program
items.summary: Physician-scientists hold a unique role in the medical field. They balance patient care with conducting research. Physician-scientists spend much of their time in laboratories performing experiments to drive innovation across a wide range of medical treatments. But their numbers are declining.

items.title: Conditions remain 'unchanged' for young people with disability, data reveal
items.summary: The level of inequity experienced by young people with disability has remained largely unchanged since the turn of the century, according to new research led by Flinders University. Using data from the Household, Income and Labor Dynamics in Australia Survey (HILDA), researchers examined the extent

items.title: JWST captures rare glimpse of early black hole growing inside network of young galaxies
items.summary: Astronomers using the James Webb Space Telescope have captured one of the clearest views yet of how an early supermassive black hole may grow within a network of young galaxies. The compact active galaxy, seen just a billion years after the Big Bang, lies beside a 12,000-parsec-long filament contain

items.title: The human side of animal health: Global review reveals new insights to achieving better biosecurity
items.summary: When it comes to protecting livestock from devastating diseases, simply telling people what to do may not be enough. A new review led by a researcher from the University of Vermont and collaborators from two institutions in Australia suggests that improving farm biosecurity requires something often

items.title: Study spotlights human toll of abrupt U.S. foreign aid cuts
items.summary: In January 2025, the Trump administration announced a 90-day freeze on all United States government (USG) foreign aid, which subsequently became a permanent shutdown of USAID in July 2025. New research finds that the abrupt cessation of foreign aid disrupted health and humanitarian systems worldwide

items.title: Antarctica may give the world decades to prepare for rising seas
items.summary: Scientists may be able to reliably forecast Antarctic ice loss through the middle of this century, offering governments a vital head start on sea level rise. After that, unstable ice retreat could accelerate dramatically, making the future much harder to predict.

items.title: Portugal's ancient giant bears shrank over thousands of years and humans may be to blame
items.summary: About 22,000 years ago, a giant brown bear lumbered across what is now Portugal. The creature weighed around 385 kg (about 850 pounds), making it one of the largest of its kind ever found, dwarfing even the largest brown bears today.

items.title: Climate change making Europe drought 'more severe': study
items.summary: Human-caused climate change has intensified a drought across Europe, with extreme heat rather than low rainfall driving the exceptionally dry conditions, a team of international scientists said Thursday.

items.title: Striped or checkered? Magnetic field influences competing electronic patterns in a graphene-like quantum material
items.summary: In most everyday materials, such as copper, silver and silicon, the behavior of electrons is relatively predictable. In quantum materials, however, electrons can interact in complex ways, giving rise to collective electronic states with remarkable properties. Understanding how these states emerge—an

items.title: National food waste recycling promises major climate gains but comes with microplastic risks
items.summary: Keeping food waste out of landfills provides climate and other environmental benefits, and Vermont requires residents to divert these materials to better management pathways. With other states considering similar policies, UVM researchers studied the potential outcomes of bringing these efforts to a

items.title: In Ghana, one man's race to save critically endangered fish
items.summary: As dawn breaks over Ghana's Atlantic coast, artisanal fishing boats return to shore with last night's catch—baskets of snapper, crabs, rays and a distinct-looking fish locals call "esene."

items.title: June heat wave cost UK economy more than £1bn: Study
items.summary: Britain's record-breaking June heat wave resulted in some 24 million lost hours of work, costing the economy £1.15 billion ($1.5 billion), according to an estimate released Thursday by the London School of Economics (LSE).

items.title: Florida researchers bring endangered elkhorn coral ashore to protect them
items.summary: Scientists working to save a coral species from extinction transported 30 pieces from nurseries off the Florida coast to a hatchery on land Wednesday, fearing that warming waters could trigger a mass bleaching event this summer that could wipe them out.

items.title: NASA will test SpaceX and Blue Origin Moon landers before the next lunar landing
items.summary: NASA is preparing for future astronaut landings on the Moon with an unusually ambitious orbital rehearsal. During Artemis III in 2027, Orion astronauts will rendezvous and dock separately with prototype lunar landers from Blue Origin and SpaceX, allowing crews and ground teams to test the complex ma

items.title: NASA’s Webb telescope discovers giant planet hiding in one of astronomy’s most famous systems
items.summary: A giant planet had been hiding inside one of astronomy’s most closely studied planetary systems, concealed by a bright disk of cosmic dust. Webb revealed Beta Pictoris d through atmospheric traces of carbon monoxide, water vapor, and methane, showcasing a promising new method for finding elusive exo

items.title: Olympic Mountain Glory
items.summary: Snow-capped mountains carved by deep river valleys preside over northwestern Washington state.

items.title: Orcas filmed ramming one of the world's heaviest fish so hard it explodes, possibly for fun
items.summary: Orcas are known to be smart and ruthless hunters. They've been observed hunting a variety of prey, with some species, such as whales, larger than themselves. They use a wide range of strategies to hunt and kill prey, but now, in the Gulf of California, researchers have observed one that hasn&#0

items.title: Study of incarcerated women in Chile finds different outcomes after release
items.summary: A new study of incarcerated women in Chile reveals that women respond to imprisonment in very different ways: While most experienced little change in their involvement in crime after release, those with higher levels of past criminal involvement transitioned toward less offending. Access to employme

items.title: Millennial minimalism: Living in a less material world
items.summary: Consumer behavior accounts for almost two-thirds of global greenhouse gas emissions. Research published in the International Journal of Business Excellence argues that reducing absolute consumption, rather than simply making it greener, needs to be a priority in environmental policy.

items.title: Natural compound may fight rheumatoid arthritis at its source
items.summary: A plant-derived compound reduced swelling, inflammation, and joint damage in rats with rheumatoid arthritis. It also helped rebalance immune activity and improve the way certain fats were processed in the body. Researchers say the findings could lead to a new approach for treating the disease.

items.title: Scientists discover the hidden brain switch that assigns bees their jobs
items.summary: Researchers found that specific brain circuits help decide which jobs bees perform as they age. When those circuits were switched off, older worker bees began caring for the queen again instead of carrying out their normal later-life duties. The findings reveal that a bee colony’s complex division o

items.title: Extreme heat wave grips Tunisia as wide power cuts fuel frustration
items.summary: In Tunisia, long power outages across the country have added to the strain of an intense heat wave, with temperatures as high as 49°C (120°F), leaving many fearing for their livelihoods.

items.title: Genome tool places large genetic sequences precisely in rice and tobacco without DNA breaks
items.summary: Researchers at King Abdullah University of Science and Technology (KAUST) have developed a new way to add large pieces of genetic information to plants, overcoming a challenge that has limited plant biotechnology for decades. The advance could help scientists build more complex traits into plants in

items.title: A longer, hotter reality surrounds marine heat waves, research reveals
items.summary: Anyone who has boiled a pot of water understands that it takes both time and energy to raise the temperature, so it may come as a surprise to learn that scientists have long treated marine heat waves (MHWs) as isolated events. New research led by William & Mary's VIMS & Batten School is challen

items.title: Marine animals first colonized empty seashells about 470 million years ago
items.summary: An international study led by researchers from the University of Tartu shows that approximately 470 million years ago, a significant ecological change took place in the world's oceans: For the first time, the interiors of empty mollusk shells began to be used as habitats. This marked the emerge

items.title: Quantum internet leaves the lab with first real-world entanglement over busy telecom fiber
items.summary: Quantum information is notoriously fragile. Internet traffic is anything but. Yet Northwestern University scientists have demonstrated they can peacefully coexist inside the same fiber-optic cable.

items.title: Light-driven chemistry method could create antiviral compound libraries 10 to 100 times larger within weeks
items.summary: As the world faces new and often untreatable viral threats, Simon Fraser University researchers have found a way to cut years off the time it takes to discover antiviral drugs. The new research enables scientists to quickly create large libraries of nucleoside analogs (NAs), compounds that mimic the

items.title: Water-surface vortices drive tiny rotors without electricity, magnets or chemicals
items.summary: Reliably generating controlled miniature rotations has long been a challenge: Chemical propulsion systems wear out, and methods that use electric or magnetic fields require complex setups. A team from KIT's Institute of Microstructure Technology (IMT) and the Suzhou Institute of Nano-tech and N

items.title: Even a slight overproduction of tubulin can tip the balance of cellular health, study shows
items.summary: For cells to function properly, they must produce the right amount of each protein. It is a delicate balance: Both too little and too much can compromise essential cellular functions. A team from the University of Geneva (UNIGE) has now shown that even a modest excess of tubulin—the protein that ass

items.title: Reusable magnetic invention removes microplastics plus some PFAS from water
items.summary: Microplastics are an increasing global concern, with growing evidence of their presence in water systems. RMIT University researchers have developed a water treatment material that rapidly removes micro- and nanoplastics and some PFAS (per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances), bringing the technology cl

items.title: Shifting weather patterns drive 55% of Europe's summer drought trend, study finds
items.summary: In a new study, researchers at Leipzig University have identified a major cause of the unusually severe summer soil drying and rising temperatures across Europe. They found that much of the trend can be attributed to changes in atmospheric circulation patterns—that is, shifts in the weather regimes

items.title: Plasma design rules show how to preserve attosecond flashes for observing electrons
items.summary: Researchers at Skoltech, together with a colleague from the Shanghai Institute of Optics and Fine Mechanics of the Chinese Academy of Sciences, working within the joint SIOM–Skoltech laboratory, have determined how to select the thickness and density of a plasma target so that a pulse passing throug

items.title: Neural networks unlock larger quantum simulations with lower computational costs
items.summary: In recent years, research using artificial intelligence to predict material properties has advanced rapidly. Neural network quantum Monte Carlo methods have attracted attention as highly accurate simulation techniques. However, their extremely high computational cost has limited their application to

items.title: Detecting the body's magnetic fields with a low-power Ramsey-based magnetometer
items.summary: Our bodies generate extremely weak magnetic fields as electric currents flow through the heart, brain and other tissues. These signals are used in magnetocardiography and magnetoencephalography to assess heart function and brain activity, respectively. These fields can be detected at room temperatur

items.title: Amerikanac tuži OpenAI zbog medicinskog savjeta ChatGPT-a: "Skoro sam umro"
items.summary: STANOVNIK Floride tuži OpenAI jer su ga krivi medicinski savjeti ChatGPT-a doveli do plućne embolije. Tužba tereti tvrtku za nemar i nadriliječništvo, a traži i povlačenje ChatGPT Healtha.

items.title: Crowd reactions influence whether people receive controversial political messages positively or negatively
items.summary: Positive crowd reactions like applause and laughter can make controversial political messages more acceptable to viewers, according to new research by psychologist Dr. Carl Senior at Aston University. The research suggests that how broadcasters and content creators use applause, laughter and booing

items.title: AI and quantum chemistry combine to identify efficient blue OLED materials
items.summary: Organic light-emitting diodes (OLEDs) have become a standard in modern devices with incredible contrast and sleek designs. While initially an expensive luxury, OLEDs are gradually becoming more financially accessible as the technology improves. Now, researchers at the Institute of Transformative Bio
